title: SEGMENT 1 — Why Illumina dominates, and what changed when sequencing got cheap

lines.text: SEGMENT 1 — Why Illumina dominates, and what changed when sequencing got cheap
Alright, let's get started.
Good morning, everyone.

Today and for the next two hours, we're going to talk about Illumina sequencing — specifically, the technology that single-handedly turned reading a human genome from a multi-billion-dollar international megaproject into a routine assay you can order online for a few hundred bucks and have results back in a couple of days.
That sentence sounds like marketing copy.
It's not.
It's an understatement of one of the most dramatic technological shifts in the history of biology.

And the engine of that shift is a single platform — sequencing by synthesis, abbreviated SBS — which Illumina perfected and now sells from instruments that range from a desktop box the size of a coffee maker to a refrigerator-sized monster that can read twenty thousand human genomes a year.
So let me start with a number that should genuinely shock you.
The Human Genome Project, which ran from 1990 to 2003, cost roughly three billion dollars and took about thirteen years to produce the first complete human genome sequence.
That's a single genome, by an international consortium of hundreds of labs, using the best technology available at the time — Sanger sequencing, which we'll come back to.
By 2014, an Illumina instrument called the HiSeq X Ten could sequence a human genome for about a thousand dollars in a couple of days, on its own.
By 2023, the NovaSeq X — Illumina's latest flagship — pushed that cost down to roughly two hundred dollars per genome.
So in twenty years, the cost of sequencing one human dropped from three billion dollars to two hundred.
That's a factor of fifteen million.
Moore's law, the famous trajectory of computer chips, looks tame next to it.
Sequencing got cheaper, faster than anything else in modern technology has ever gotten cheaper.
And the reason is Illumina.
Here's the analogy I want you to hold in your head for the next two hours.
Imagine you wanted to figure out, letter by letter, the contents of a very long book — say, three billion letters long, which is roughly the size of the human genome.

The old way of doing it, Sanger sequencing, was like reading the book carefully with a single very precise person sitting at a desk, taking their time, reading one section at a time, then moving on to the next.
The careful reader is accurate — they don't make many mistakes — but they're slow, and they're expensive, and if you want to read the whole book you have to wait.

Illumina sequencing, by contrast, is like ripping the book into millions of tiny scraps — each scrap a couple of sentences long — pasting all the scraps onto a glass slide, and then having millions of tiny readers, each one assigned to one scrap, all reading their scrap at the same time while a camera takes a photograph every few seconds to record what each reader has just read.
Millions of tiny sequencing reactions, happening side by side on a glass slide, all watched by a camera that takes a photo after each cycle.
That's the picture.
Hold it.
The key word in that sentence is parallelism.
Sanger sequencing reads one fragment of DNA at a time.
Illumina reads, depending on the instrument, somewhere between a few million and a few billion fragments simultaneously, all on the same surface, in the same run.
That parallelism is where all the speed and all the cost reduction come from.
The chemistry of reading any single fragment, on an Illumina instrument, is not dramatically faster than reading a single fragment with Sanger.
It's actually slower per base.
But because you're reading ten billion fragments at the same time instead of one, the total throughput is something like ten billion times higher.
That's the trick.

If you walk out of here in two hours and you can do three things — one, sketch the four-step cycle of Illumina sequencing on a whiteboard and explain what each step does and why; two, explain in plain English why bridge amplification and clusters are necessary, given how dim a single molecule's signal is; three, look at any modern genomics paper that says "Illumina sequencing was performed" and have a real intuition for what that means about cost, read length, error rates, and what the data
looks like — then we've succeeded.
Everything else hangs off those three.
One more orienting fact before we go deeper.

Illumina, the company, controls something like seventy to eighty percent of the global next-generation sequencing market — and at various times in the last decade that share has been higher than ninety percent.

When you read a paper that says "we sequenced this with Illumina" or "we performed RNA-seq" or "we did whole-exome sequencing on these patients," what you're almost certainly reading about is data produced by an Illumina machine.
The big competitors — and we'll meet a few of them in Segment 16 — are real but small.
For now, when I say "next-generation sequencing," I mean, in the overwhelming majority of cases, Illumina.

The platform is so dominant that "Illumina-style data" has become a kind of generic format that the rest of bioinformatics is built around — short reads, paired-end, with quality scores attached, in files called FASTQ.
The whole downstream ecosystem of variant callers, aligners, expression quantifiers, and analysis tools was shaped by what Illumina produces.
So that's the contract.

We're going to spend two hours on one platform — the platform — and we're going to do it the way you'd want to understand any dominant technology: by getting the chemistry right, then the engineering, then the economics, then the failure modes, then the competitive landscape.
Let's start with where it came from.
